task: I’m getting my tires rotated soon and want to make sure I’m not overlooking anything basic. Can you help me figure out what I should check before I drive off, and whether there’s anything specific I should ask them to verify while it’s on the lift?

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — a quick tire rotation is a good time to catch a few basic issues.

## Before you drive off, check:
- **Tire pressure**: Make sure all four tires are set to the correct PSI, not just “close enough.”
- **Lug nuts**: Confirm they were properly tightened. If you want to be extra safe, ask whether they used a torque wrench.
- **Tire rotation pattern**: Ask which tires were moved where, so you can keep track of wear.
- **TPMS light**: Make sure the tire pressure warning light isn’t on afterward.
- **Steering feel / vibrations**: On the drive home, note whether the car pulls, shakes, or feels different.
- **Wheel covers / center caps**: Make sure nothing is loose, missing, or damaged.

## While it’s on the lift, good things to ask them to verify:
- **Tread depth and uneven wear**: Look for cupping, feathering, inside-edge wear, or one tire wearing much faster.
- **Tire damage**: Cuts, bulges, punctures, sidewall cracks, or embedded objects.
- **Brake condition**: Pad thickness and rotor condition are easy to inspect while the wheels are off.
- **Suspension/steering components**: Ask them to look for loose tie rods, ball joints, control arm bushings, or leaking shocks/struts.
- **Wheel condition**: Bent rims, corrosion on the bead, or signs of a slow leak.
- **Wheel balance / alignment clues**: Uneven wear can suggest alignment or balance issues.
- **Valve stems / TPMS sensors**: Check for cracks, leaks, or damage.

## A few smart questions to ask:
- “Can you show me the tread depth on each tire?”
- “Do you see any uneven wear that suggests an alignment issue?”
- “Any signs of a slow leak or wheel damage?”
- “Are the lug nuts torqued to spec?”
- “Do you recommend alignment or balancing based on what you saw?”

If you want, I can also give you a **simple 30-second tire rotation checklist** you can use at the shop.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, this is mostly right. I’d want one more layer on it though — the stuff that actually gets missed.

Before I leave:
- ask for the **torque spec** they used, not just “tightened”
- make sure they **reset TPMS** if the car needs it
- check the **spare** too if you’ve got one, since people forget it
- if they swapped fronts to rear, note whether they told you about **directional tires** or staggered setup
- look at the **lug nut seats** and wheel faces for damage if they had a rough time getting them off

On the lift, I’d specifically ask them to check:
- **inner tire wear** — that’s the part people don’t see and alignment problems show up there first
- **uneven brake wear** left to right
- **sticking caliper / seized slide pins** if one wheel looks hotter or more worn
- **dry rot / sidewall cracking** if the tires are older
- **wheel bearing play** if there’s any noise or wobble

And honestly, I’d ask them to tell you if they see anything that would make a rotation pointless soon anyway. Last time I had a shop just rotate and send it, then I found one tire was already wearing funny because of a bad sensor setup on the car — wasted time. Better to catch the real issue while it’s up.

If you want, I can turn this into a short shop script you can literally read off your phone.

turns.agent_response:
Yep — that’s the better version.

Here’s a tighter **“don’t-miss-the-obvious”** list:

### Before you leave
- **Ask for the torque spec** they used, not just “tightened.”
- **Confirm TPMS reset** if your car requires it.
- **Check the spare** too, if you have one.
- **Ask about tire type/setup** if applicable:
  - **Directional tires**: can only go certain ways
  - **Staggered setup**: fronts/rears may not be interchangeable
- **Inspect lug nut seats and wheel faces** for damage, especially if anything fought them coming off.
- **Note the rotation pattern** so you know what was moved where.

### While it’s on the lift
Ask them to specifically check:
- **Inner tire wear** and uneven wear patterns
- **Brake wear side-to-side**
- **Sticking caliper / seized slide pins**
- **Dry rot / sidewall cracking**
- **Wheel bearing play** or wobble
- **Any sign the rotation won’t help much** because a bigger issue is already there

### Good quick ask to the shop
> “Can you check the inner wear, brake wear, and anything that would make the rotation not worth it yet?”

That usually gets them looking for the stuff that actually gets missed.

If you want, I can make this into a **5-line phone script** you can use at the counter.

user_simulator.text: Yeah, that’s basically the version I’d use. The 5-line script would be the useful part, honestly.
